comments.text:
It is well known that the existence of arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions of consecutive primes (CPAPs) is open unconditionally. The purpose of this post is to record a clean and explicit argument showing that the conjecture follows conditionally from Schinzel’s Hypothesis H (equivalently, Dickson’s conjecture for linear forms), using a Chinese Remainder Theorem construction to force all intermediate terms to be composite. This implication is generally regarded as folklore; variants appear implicitly or explicitly in several places (see references below). I am posting this mainly as a self contained exposition. Conditional theorem (Hypothesis H). Assume Schinzel’s Hypothesis H (prime values of admissible systems of linear forms). Then for every $k \ge 3$, there exist infinitely many $k$-term arithmetic progressions of consecutive primes. Construction. Fix $k \ge 3$, and let\[ d = k^\# = \prod_{p \le k} p \]be the primorial. Define the set of “between-term offsets” $B := { r : 1 \le r \le (k-1)d - 1,\ d 
mid r }$. For each $r \in B$, choose a distinct prime $q_r > (k-1)d$, and set $M := \prod_{r \in B} q_r$. By the Chinese Remainder Theorem, choose an integer $b$ such that $b + r \equiv 0 \pmod{q_r}$ for all $r \in B$, and $b 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod p$ for every prime $p \le k$. Define linear forms $L_j(n) := Mn + b + jd$ for $j = 0,1,\dots,k-1$. Properties. Forced compositeness between terms. For any $r \in B$, $Mn + b + r \equiv 0 \pmod{q_r}$, so every integer strictly between consecutive terms of the progression is composite. Admissibility. The system ${L_0,\dots,L_{k-1}}$ has no fixed prime divisor: – If $p \le k$, then $p \mid d$ and $L_j(n) \equiv b 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod p$. – If $p \mid M$, then $p = q_r$ for some $r \in B$, and $L_j(n) \equiv b + jd \equiv -r + jd 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod p$, since $q_r > (k-1)d$. – If $p > k$ and $p 
mid M$, there are at most $k < p$ forbidden residue classes of $n \pmod p$. Prime values. By Schinzel’s Hypothesis H, there exist infinitely many $n$ such that all $L_j(n)$ are prime. Conclusion. For such $n$, the primes $L_0(n), L_1(n), \dots, L_{k-1}(n)$ form an arithmetic progression of length $k$ with common difference $d$, and every integer between consecutive terms is composite. Hence they are $k$ consecutive primes. Thus, conditional on Hypothesis H, CPAPs of arbitrary length exist. References / attribution. Schinzel–Sierpiński (1958), Sur certaines hypothèses concernant les nombres premiers, Acta Arith. Dubner–Forbes–Lygeros–Mizony–Nelson–Zimmermann (2001), Consecutive primes in arithmetic progression, Math. Comp. MathOverflow discussion noting the conditional implication as folklore: https://mathoverflow.net/questions/187096/
If $p\leq k$ then surely $L_j(n) \equiv Mn+b\mod p$.

It's worth noting that if the Hardy-Littlewood prime $k$-tuples conjecture is true, then if $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is admissible, then there are infinitely many translations of $A$ into sets of consecutive primes. To see this, let $n_1$ be an element that is in between elements of $A$ but not in $A$ itself, and let $k_1$ be larger than $|A|$. Then we can construct a set $B_1$ by adding elements larger than those in $A$ in a way that preserves admissibility and occupies every residue class of $k_1$ except for the residue class of $n_1$. Then, let $n_2$ be another element in between elements of $A$ that's not in $A$ itself, let $k_2 > |A \cup B_1|$, create $B_2$ similarly, and repeat until each $n_i$ is taken care of. Let $B$ be the union of all $B_i$. Then the elements of $B$ are all greater than the elements in $A$, $A \cup B$ is admissible, and (with at most finitely many exceptions) any translation of $A \cup B$ into the primes cannot have any primes in between elements of $A$ except for the elements of $A$ themselves. If the prime $k$-tuples conjecture is true, then $A \cup B$ has infinitely many translations into the primes, which completes the proof. (Side note: I wouldn't be surprised if this argument already appears in the literature somewhere; please do let me know if that's the case. EDIT: Dogmachine has found a reference - thanks!)
@DesmondWeisenberg, this was certainly shown by Schinzel and Sierpinski in the 1958 paper, Sur certaines hypothèses concernant les nombres premiers.
